planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; honeybush grows rapidly and vigorously in warm conditions and can reach 6–8 feet in a single season. In zone 8–9 it dies back to the roots in hard winters but resprouts vigorously the following spring. The leaves have a strong, pungent smell when brushed — sometimes described as peanut-butter — which is distinctive but not universally pleasant.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; honeybush is one of the most dramatically architectural foliage plants available for warm-climate gardens, native to South Africa and producing enormous, pinnate leaves of a gray-blue to blue-green color with serrated leaflets and a distinctive, almost metallic, blue-gray bloom — like the foliage of a tropical acacia crossed with a glaucous eucalyptus. The blue-gray color is among the most striking leaf colors in any plant and provides a spectacular contrast to darker- or brighter-foliaged plants. In late winter to early spring it produces erect spikes of deep-maroon to brick-red tubular flowers that produce copious, sweet, honey-like nectar — giving rise to both the common name and the species name (melianthus = honey flower in Greek). The flowers are primarily pollinated by sunbirds in South Africa and attract hummingbirds in North America. Honeybush is widely grown in coastal California, the Gulf Coast, and mild British gardens as a bold, exotic foliage specimen for large borders, containers, and tropical-style plantings.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, fertile to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; spiny acanthus is more drought-tolerant and more deeply cut in its foliage than soft acanthus (A. mollis) — it is also more heat-tolerant and better adapted to dry summer conditions. Wear gloves when handling as the leaf tips bear sharp spines.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile to average soil in full sun to part shade; spiny acanthus is the superior species for hot-summer and dry-summer gardens — the deeply lobed, spiny-tipped, dark-green leaves are more dramatic in outline than soft acanthus (A. mollis), and the plant is more cold-hardy, more drought-tolerant, and more adaptable to a wide range of conditions. The leaves inspired the capital designs of Corinthian columns in classical Greek and Roman architecture, one of the most ancient uses of any plant in decorative art. In summer, spectacular vertical spikes 3–5 feet tall arise carrying hooded, two-toned flowers: purple-maroon bracts surrounding white flowers with prominent spiny bracts between each flower. These spikes are among the most architecturally impressive flower structures of any perennial and the flowers are visited by bumblebees that are strong enough to push into the hooded blooms. Once established, acanthus spreads by thick underground roots and can be difficult to eradicate — site it where it can expand freely.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; beaked yucca requires excellent drainage and is killed by waterlogged soils. In humid Gulf Coast conditions, plant on raised berms or in rocky, fast-draining soil to prevent crown rot. It is one of the most cold-hardy of the tree-type yuccas and is reliably cold-hardy to zone 5 with proper drainage.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; beaked yucca is widely considered the most ornamental of the tree yuccas — a slow-growing, single-stemmed Texas native from the Trans-Pecos that develops over many years into a remarkable blue-leaved specimen with a trunk to 10 feet or more, crowned by a perfect, symmetrical pom-pom of hundreds of narrow, powder-blue, rigid leaves that radiate in all directions. The color is among the most intensely glaucous blue-gray of any yucca, creating an outstanding specimen plant with a bold, sculptural presence. In late spring, a tall flower spike emerges from the center bearing a large, dense panicle of white, bell-shaped flowers that are attractive to yucca moths and other pollinators. Native to the Chihuahuan Desert limestone mountains of west Texas and northern Mexico, it is cold-hardy to zone 5 where drainage is excellent. Outstanding as a container specimen, landscape specimen, and architectural focal point — slow-growing and long-lived.

planting.note: Plant in very well-drained, lean, rocky or gritty, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Queen Victoria agave requires excellent drainage above all else and is killed by wet, poorly drained soil. It is among the hardiest agave species, tolerating temperatures to 10°F or below when dry. In humid climates, plant in raised, well-drained beds with gravel mulch to prevent crown rot.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, gritty, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Queen Victoria agave is generally considered the most beautiful of all agaves — a slow-growing, compact species from the limestone cliffs of the Chihuahuan Desert in Nuevo León and Coahuila, Mexico, producing a dense, geometrically perfect rosette of short, stiff, dark-green leaves with distinctive white edges and markings that create an intricate, almost hand-drawn pattern unique to this species. The narrow, closely packed leaves with their crisp white margins and patterning look sculpted and architectural rather than plant-like. Being monocarpic (flowering once and dying), it produces a magnificent flower spike reaching 10–15 feet after many years (10–30 or more), producing creamy-white flowers before the mother plant dies and offsets develop to continue the colony. Hardy to 10°F when dry, it is suitable for zone 7 in well-drained, rocky situations. A prized collector's plant and exceptional container specimen.

planting.note: Plant grafted or layered plants (NOT seedlings) in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Chinese wisteria requires a very strong, permanent support — the vines can reach 100 lbs per linear foot on mature, established plants. INVASIVE: Chinese wisteria is a Category I invasive pest plant in the southeastern United States, escaping cultivation and smothering native forest understories. Consider the non-invasive native alternatives Wisteria frutescens or W. macrostachya instead.

directions: Plant grafted plants in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun with very strong permanent support; Chinese wisteria is one of the most spectacular flowering vines in temperate gardening — in full bloom in April and May the plant is covered with cascading, pendulous clusters (racemes) of fragrant, violet-purple flowers up to 12 inches long that open all at once in a breathtaking floral display. The fragrance is sweet and pervasive. However, Chinese wisteria is classified as an invasive exotic pest plant throughout the southeastern United States, where it has escaped cultivation to smother trees and native forest understory in a manner similar to kudzu. It twines clockwise (Japanese wisteria, W. floribunda, twines counterclockwise). The vines become massive with age — potentially requiring structural reinforcement of pergolas and arbors. For gardeners seeking wisteria bloom without ecological risk, the native American wisterias (Wisteria frutescens, W. macrostachya) offer similar floral display with smaller racemes and WITHOUT the invasive risk. Confirm wisteria regulations in your state before planting Chinese or Japanese wisteria.
